title: Component Library
description: Creative palette of 28 components across 6 categories (Content, Explore, Assess, Layout, Media, Structure) for designing premium deep-scroll learning experiences. Each entry describes the learning moment it creates, creative uses beyond the obvious, and technical props. The generation agent should treat this as an inspiration deck, not a checklist.

components.description: Full-viewport opening section with animated title, optional background image, and scroll-down indicator.
components.learningMoment: The hero is the learner's first impression — it answers 'Why should I care about this?' in three seconds. A great hero creates anticipation. A boring one triggers the back button.
creativeUses: Pose a provocative question as the subtitle instead of a description
Use a surprising statistic as the title itself ('80% of breaches start with one email')
Frame the course as a challenge: 'Can you spot a phishing email before it spots you?'
components.when_to_use: Always use as the FIRST component. Every course starts with a hero.
components.when_not_to_use: Never use more than once per course.

variants.name: centered-overlay
variants.when_to_use: Default. Strong hero image, punchy title. Works for any topic.
variants.when_not_to_use: Never wrong, but predictable if used every time.

variants.name: split-screen
variants.when_to_use: When the hero image is strong and specific (not abstract). Creates a magazine feel. Text left, image right, 50/50 grid.
variants.when_not_to_use: Weak without a real image. Don't use if the image would be generic.

variants.name: minimal-text
variants.when_to_use: Abstract or conceptual topics. When typography IS the design. No image, border-left accent, bold typography only. Creates sophistication.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use if you have a great hero image — it would be wasted.

required.displayTitle: string — Course title, large animated text
required.body: string (HTML) — Subtitle or course description, 1-2 sentences

_graphic.large: string — URL for background image (16:9, will be overlaid with dark scrim)
_graphic.alt: string — Alt text for the image

example.body: Master the fundamentals of EV technology, safety protocols, and maintenance best practices.

_graphic.alt: Modern electric vehicle at a charging station

components.description: Clean text block with heading and body. The workhorse component for explanatory content.
components.learningMoment: Text sets the stage. It's where you build the narrative thread that connects everything else. The best text blocks make the learner nod and think 'tell me more' — not 'I know this already.'
creativeUses: Open with a counterintuitive claim that the rest of the section will prove
Use it as a narrative bridge between interactive components — the connective tissue
Write a 'what if' scenario that sets up the next component
components.when_to_use: For introductory paragraphs, explanations, context-setting text, or any content that is primarily prose. Works well as the first block in a section to set context before interactive components.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for content that would benefit from visual structure (lists → checklist, steps → timeline, definitions → key-term).

variants.name: standard
variants.when_to_use: Default. Single-column prose with heading. Clean, readable, works everywhere.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel monotonous if every text block uses this.

variants.name: two-column
variants.when_to_use: Split body text into two columns for scannable content — comparisons, parallel points, or dense reference material. Creates a magazine feel.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for narrative prose that flows linearly. Don't use for short text (needs enough content to fill both columns).

variants.name: highlight-box
variants.when_to_use: Subtle background card with left accent border. Elevates the text visually — good for key takeaways, summary paragraphs, or important context.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for every text block — loses impact. Reserve for 1-2 per course that deserve emphasis.

required.body:
string (HTML) — Main content. Can include

,

    ,
      ,

      , , tags.

optional.displayTitle: string — Section heading above the body text
optional.instruction: string — Italic helper text (e.g. 'Read the following carefully')

example.body: Electric vehicles replace the internal combustion engine with an electric motor powered by a rechargeable battery pack. This fundamental difference affects every aspect of how the vehicle is maintained, repaired, and handled in emergencies.

components.description: Full-width image with hover zoom effect and optional caption. Provides visual breathing room between text-heavy sections.
components.learningMoment: A well-placed image lets the brain process what it just read. It's not decoration — it's cognitive breathing room. The best standalone images make the learner pause and look closer.
creativeUses: Use after a dense accordion or timeline as a visual reset
Pair with a provocative caption that reframes what the image shows
Place an infographic-style image that summarizes the preceding section
components.when_to_use: For standalone images that deserve full attention — diagrams, photographs, infographics. Place between text sections to break up content rhythm.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for images paired with explanatory text (use graphic-text instead). Don't use for decorative images with no educational value.

variants.name: standard
variants.when_to_use: Default. Full-width image with hover zoom. Clean, simple presentation.
variants.when_not_to_use: If the image needs a prominent caption, use captioned-card.

variants.name: captioned-card
variants.when_to_use: Image presented inside a card with rounded corners, shadow, and a visible caption area below. Better for diagrams or infographics that need context.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for atmospheric or background-style images — the card frame reduces impact.

_graphic.alt: Cutaway diagram showing battery cell arrangement in an EV chassis

example.body: A typical EV battery pack contains thousands of individual cells arranged in modules.

components.description: Side-by-side split layout with text on one side and image on the other. Alternates left/right for visual rhythm.
components.learningMoment: The human eye naturally moves between image and text, creating a visual dialogue. Each graphic-text is a mini-story: the image catches attention, the text delivers understanding.
creativeUses: Use the image to show the 'before' and the text to explain the 'after'
Pair a real-world photo with an explanation of what's actually happening
Show a person/scenario and use the text to tell their story
components.when_to_use: For content that pairs an explanation with a supporting image. Alternate _imageAlign between sections for visual variety.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use back-to-back more than twice. If the image isn't directly related to the text, use separate text + graphic components.

variants.name: split
variants.when_to_use: Default 50/50 side-by-side with glow effect on image. Works for any paired content.
variants.when_not_to_use: Predictable if every graphic-text uses this.

variants.name: overlap
variants.when_to_use: 60% image with text card overlapping from the right. Elevated, premium feel. Great for feature highlights or case studies.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use without a strong image — the overlap draws attention to the image.

variants.name: full-overlay
variants.when_to_use: Full-width image with gradient overlay, text positioned on one side. Image is the star. Best when the image tells half the story.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for long body text — the overlay limits reading space.

required.displayTitle: string — Heading for the text side

optional._imageAlign: string — 'left' or 'right' (default: right). Alternate for visual rhythm.

example.body:
When you lift your foot off the accelerator, the electric motor reverses its role — becoming a generator that converts kinetic energy back into electrical energy stored in the battery.

This process can recover up to 70% of braking energy, extending range significantly.

_graphic.alt: Diagram showing energy flow during regenerative braking

components.description: Expandable panels with smooth animation and completion tracking. Each panel has a title and rich body content revealed on click.
components.learningMoment: Accordions create curiosity gaps — the learner sees the titles and wonders what's inside. The act of clicking to reveal is a micro-commitment to learning. Use titles that make people WANT to click.
creativeUses: Myth vs Reality: each panel title is a myth, the body reveals the truth
Role-based exploration: 'If you're a manager...', 'If you're an IC...'
Progressive complexity: panels go from basic to advanced, learner self-selects depth
FAQ format: real questions learners have, with thorough answers
components.when_to_use: For content with 3-8 distinct topics of roughly equal weight that learners can explore in any order.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for sequential content (use narrative or timeline). Don't use with fewer than 3 items. Don't use for critical content that ALL learners must see (it's optional to expand).

variants.name: standard
variants.when_to_use: Default. Clean panels with border and chevron icon. Works for any accordion content.
variants.when_not_to_use: Predictable if every accordion uses this.

variants.name: accent-border
variants.when_to_use: Left border accent alternating primary/secondary colours with Material Symbols icons in summary. More visual distinction between panels. Great for myths vs reality, categorised content.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use if the accordion items don't have a natural icon mapping.

required._items: array — 3-8 accordion panels, each with { title: string, body: string (HTML) }

optional.displayTitle: string — Section heading above the accordion
optional.body: string (HTML) — Intro text above the panels
optional.instruction: string — Helper text (e.g. 'Select each panel to learn more')

example.instruction: Select each panel to explore the safety features.

_items.title: High-Voltage Isolation
_items.body: EV battery packs operate at 400-800V. Multiple isolation barriers prevent current from reaching the vehicle chassis or occupants.

_items.title: Battery Management System (BMS)
_items.body: The BMS continuously monitors cell temperature, voltage, and current. It can disconnect the battery in milliseconds if anomalies are detected.

_items.title: Thermal Runaway Protection
_items.body: Modern EVs use liquid cooling, fire-resistant barriers between cells, and venting systems to prevent and contain thermal events.

components.description: Multiple-choice quiz with selection, submit, visual feedback, and retry. Supports single and multi-select modes.
components.learningMoment: The best quiz questions create a moment of productive doubt — the learner thinks they know, commits to an answer, and then discovers whether their understanding holds up. That moment of uncertainty is where learning happens.
creativeUses: Scenario-based: 'Your colleague clicks a suspicious link. What's your FIRST action?'
Myth-busting: present a common misconception as a plausible answer
Application: test whether learners can APPLY a framework, not just recall it
Prediction: 'What do you think happened next?' before revealing a case study outcome
components.when_to_use: For knowledge checks that test comprehension and application, not trivia recall. The generation agent creates ALL assessments — there are no pre-built quiz questions in the knowledge base.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't cluster multiple quizzes together. Don't test trivial recall. Don't place before teaching the material.

variants.name: stacked
variants.when_to_use: Default. Vertical list of choices in a card. Better for long answer text or detailed scenarios.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel monotonous if every quiz uses this.

variants.name: grid
variants.when_to_use: 2x2 grid with large letter labels (A/B/C/D), question centered above. Better for short answer text (under 15 words each). Creates a more visual, game-like feel.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use with long answer text — cards will overflow. Don't use with more than 4 choices.

required._items: array — Answer choices: [{ text: string, _shouldBeSelected: boolean }]

optional.instruction: string — Helper text (e.g. 'Choose the best answer')
optional._selectable: number — How many choices can be selected (default: 1). Use >1 for multi-select.

example.body: What is the typical voltage range of an EV battery pack?

_feedback.correct: Correct! Most modern EVs operate in the 400-800V range, with some performance vehicles reaching even higher.

_incorrect.final: Not quite. EV battery packs typically operate at 400-800V, which is why special safety protocols are essential.

components.description: Carousel slider with prev/next navigation, dot indicators, and optional images per slide. Content is presented sequentially.
components.learningMoment: Narratives create forward momentum — each slide builds on the last, pulling the learner through a story. The 'next' button is a promise: there's more to discover.
creativeUses: Tell a real incident as a story: slide 1 = setup, slide 2 = what went wrong, slide 3 = consequences, slide 4 = lessons learned
Before/during/after sequence showing how something changes over time
Day-in-the-life walkthrough of a role or process
Progressive reveal: each slide adds a layer of complexity to a concept
components.when_to_use: For sequential content that tells a story or walks through a process step by step. Use 3-7 slides.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for content that isn't sequential. Don't use with fewer than 3 slides.

variants.name: image-focused
variants.when_to_use: Default. Large image area (60%+) with text overlay or sidebar. Best when slides have strong visuals that tell the story.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use if slides lack images or have generic stock photos.

variants.name: text-focused
variants.when_to_use: Larger text area with optional small image. Best for story-driven narratives where the writing carries the content — case studies, incident walkthroughs.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use if you have strong images that should be the focus.

required._items: array — 3-7 slides: [{ title: string, body: string (HTML), _graphic?: { large: string, alt: string } }]

optional.displayTitle: string — Section heading above the slider
optional.body: string (HTML) — Intro text above the slider

_items.title: Step 1: Scene Assessment
_items.body: Before approaching the vehicle, assess the scene for immediate hazards: fire, fluid leaks, structural damage, or downed power lines.

_graphic.alt: First responder assessing an EV accident scene

_items.title: Step 2: Disable the Vehicle
_items.body: Locate and activate the high-voltage disconnect. This is typically found in the engine bay or under the rear seat.

_items.title: Step 3: Establish Safe Zone
_items.body: Create a 10-metre exclusion zone around the vehicle. Ensure all personnel wear appropriate PPE including insulated gloves rated to 1000V.

_graphic.alt: Exclusion zone diagram around damaged EV

components.description: Multi-card grid layout with optional image backgrounds. First card can span 2 columns for visual hierarchy. Cards have hover effects.
components.learningMoment: Bento grids let learners scan and choose what catches their eye first — it's non-linear exploration. The visual variety of cards with different content creates a sense of richness and discovery.
creativeUses: Feature showcase: each card is a different capability or benefit
Tool kit: each card is a method, technique, or resource the learner can use
Warning signs: each card describes a red flag to watch for
Quick reference: each card is a self-contained micro-lesson
components.when_to_use: For presenting 3-6 related concepts, features, or topics at a glance. Perfect for peer items that aren't sequential.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for sequential content (use narrative or timeline). Don't use with fewer than 3 items.

variants.name: grid-4
variants.when_to_use: Default. 4-column grid with featured first card spanning 2 cols. Works for 4-6 items of mixed importance.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel cramped with very long card text.

variants.name: wide-2
variants.when_to_use: 2-column layout with icon circles and more text space. Better for 3-4 items with detailed descriptions.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use with 5+ items — too many rows.

variants.name: featured
variants.when_to_use: One large featured card (gradient background, col-span-2, row-span-2) plus smaller cards. Use when one item is clearly more important than the others.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use if all items are equally important.

required._items: array — 3-6 cards: [{ title: string, body: string (HTML), _graphic?: { large: string, src: string, alt: string } }]

optional.displayTitle: string — Section heading above the grid
optional.body: string (HTML) — Intro text above the grid

_items.title: Level 1 — Home Outlet
_items.body: Standard 120V outlet. Adds 5-8km of range per hour. Best for overnight charging at home.

_items.title: Level 2 — Dedicated Charger
_items.body: 240V installation. Adds 40-50km of range per hour. Ideal for home and workplace charging.

_items.title: DC Fast Charging
_items.body: Direct current at 50-350kW. Can charge to 80% in 20-40 minutes. Found at public charging stations.

components.description: Auto-formatted data table with sticky header, alternating rows, and monospace number styling.
components.learningMoment: Tables turn scattered information into patterns. When a learner sees data organised in columns, they naturally start comparing and drawing conclusions — that's analytical thinking in action.
creativeUses: Decision matrix: criteria in rows, options in columns, with scores or ratings
Quick-reference card: condensed facts learners might bookmark or screenshot
Severity/classification matrix: map categories to outcomes
Specification comparison: technical details side by side
components.when_to_use: For comparative data, specifications, reference tables, or any structured information with consistent columns.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for simple two-column comparisons (use comparison instead). Don't use for narrative content forced into table format.

variants.name: standard
variants.when_to_use: Default. Clean table with sticky header and alternating row shading. Works for any tabular data.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel utilitarian for premium courses.

variants.name: striped-card
variants.when_to_use: Table rendered as a card with stronger visual styling — rounded corners, elevated shadow, bold header row. Better for specification tables or reference cards the learner might screenshot.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for very wide tables with many columns — card padding reduces available width.

required.body:
string (HTML) — Table data as
    /
  • items or

    lines, with columns separated by ' - ', ' : ', or ' | '

optional.displayTitle: string — Table title (shown as sticky header)

example.body:
• Battery Capacity | 75 kWh
  • Voltage | 400V
  • Weight | 450 kg
  • Warranty | 8 years / 160,000 km
  • Cycle Life | 1,500+ cycles

components.description: Video player with custom play/pause overlay, poster image, and hover controls bar.
components.learningMoment: Video creates emotional connection that text can't — facial expressions, tone of voice, real environments. It's the closest to 'being there' a digital course can get.
creativeUses: Expert interview that brings credibility to the topic
Demonstration of a procedure or technique
Real-world footage that grounds abstract concepts
components.when_to_use: Only when actual video files exist in the content bucket's media inventory.
components.when_not_to_use: Never fabricate video sources. Only reference videos that exist.

example.body: Watch how the battery management system responds to a simulated fault condition.

components.description: Multi-field form with labeled text inputs and submit button. Used for reflection prompts or data entry activities.
components.learningMoment: Writing forces thinking. When learners put their thoughts into words, they move from passive understanding to active ownership. The reflection is the learning — the form is just the container.
creativeUses: Personal action plan: 'What will you do differently on Monday?'
Pre-mortem: 'What could go wrong if you ignore this?'
Application exercise: 'Describe how this applies to YOUR situation'
Commitment device: 'Write one thing you'll change this week'
components.when_to_use: For reflective exercises where learners type their own responses. Place near the end of sections or the course for consolidation.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for assessed content (use mcq). Don't use for simple yes/no questions.

required._items: array — Form fields: [{ prefix: string (label), placeholder?: string }]

example.body: Based on what you've learned, document your key takeaways and next steps.

_items.placeholder: What stood out most to you?

_items.prefix: How will you apply this in your role?

_items.placeholder: What would you like to explore further?

components.description: Selectable option cards with letter labels (A, B, C...) and selection indicator. For scenario-based choices or self-assessment.
components.learningMoment: Branching forces the learner to commit to a decision before seeing the outcome. That moment of choice — 'What would I actually do?' — is more powerful than any amount of telling.
creativeUses: Ethical dilemma: present a grey-area scenario with no perfect answer
'What would you do?' real-world scenario before revealing the expert recommendation
Self-assessment: 'Which of these best describes your current practice?'
Priority ranking: 'Which action should come FIRST?'
components.when_to_use: For scenario-based learning where learners choose an option. Good for decision-making exercises and self-assessment.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for graded questions (use mcq). Don't use with more than 5 options.

variants.name: cards
variants.when_to_use: Default. Large selectable cards with letter labels (A, B, C). Visual, engaging, game-like feel.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel heavy with 5 options.

variants.name: list
variants.when_to_use: Compact list format with radio-button style selection. Better for longer option text or when space is tight.
variants.when_not_to_use: Less visually engaging — don't use when you want impact.

required.displayTitle: string — The scenario or question heading
required.body:
string (HTML) — Options as
  • items or

    paragraphs (parsed into cards)

example.body:
• Immediately approach the vehicle to check for injuries
  • Call emergency services and establish a safe perimeter
  • Attempt to disconnect the battery yourself
  • Wait for the vehicle manufacturer's emergency response team

components.description: Numbered steps connected by a vertical line. Each step has a number badge, title, and body content. Scroll-triggered reveal animation.
components.learningMoment: Timelines make sequence visible. When learners see steps connected by a line, they internalise the ORDER as part of the knowledge — not just what to do, but what comes first, second, third.
creativeUses: Evolution of a concept: how something changed over time (history → present → future)
Escalation path: what happens when you go from level 1 to level 5
Workflow: the steps in a real process someone follows
Cause and effect chain: A leads to B leads to C
components.when_to_use: For sequential processes, historical timelines, or step-by-step procedures where ORDER matters. Use 3-8 steps.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for non-sequential content (use accordion or bento). Don't use for very short steps with just a title (use checklist).

variants.name: vertical
variants.when_to_use: Default. Left-border with glowing dots. Works for any number of steps (3-8). Best for 5+ items.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel long with 7+ items.

variants.name: centered-alternating
variants.when_to_use: Center line, items alternate left/right with numbered circles. More visual, magazine-like feel. Best for 3-5 items.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use with 6+ items — becomes very tall. Don't use on mobile-heavy audiences (falls back to vertical).

required._items: array — 3-8 steps: [{ title: string, body: string (HTML) }]

optional.body: string (HTML) — Intro text above the timeline

_items.title: Monthly Check
_items.body: Inspect tyre pressure, washer fluid, and charge cable condition. Check for any dashboard warning lights.

_items.title: Every 6 Months
_items.body: Rotate tyres, inspect brake pads (they last longer on EVs due to regenerative braking), and check coolant levels.

_items.title: Annual Service
_items.body: Full diagnostic scan, cabin air filter replacement, battery health check, and software updates.

_items.title: Every 2 Years
_items.body: Replace brake fluid, inspect suspension components, and check high-voltage cable insulation integrity.

components.description: Side-by-side comparison columns with check/cross icons for features. Clean, scannable format for comparing two or three options.
components.learningMoment: Comparison tables trigger analytical thinking — the learner's eye bounces between columns, spotting differences and forming judgements. The check/cross pattern makes conclusions feel self-discovered rather than told.
creativeUses: Myth vs Reality: misconceptions on one side, truth on the other
Before/After: old way vs new way, showing improvement
Good/Bad practice: what to do vs what not to do
Role comparison: what's different for each role or context
components.when_to_use: For comparing 2-3 options, plans, approaches, or technologies across multiple criteria.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for data-heavy tables (use data-table). Don't compare more than 3 columns.

variants.name: columns
variants.when_to_use: Default. Side-by-side table with check/cross icons. Classic comparison format.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can look plain if used repeatedly.

variants.name: stacked-rows
variants.when_to_use: Horizontal rows with category label in center, options on each side. More compact, scannable. Better for 2-column comparisons.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use with 3 columns — only works for 2-column comparisons.

required.columns: array — 2-3 column definitions: [{ title: string, subtitle?: string }]
required.rows: array — Comparison rows: [{ label: string, values: array of (true | false | string) per column }]

components.description: Large prominent numbers or statistics with labels and optional context. Animated count-up on scroll. Displayed in a row of 2-4 stats.
components.learningMoment: Big numbers create emotional impact before the brain even processes the words. The count-up animation forces the eye to watch the number grow — building tension and making the final value land harder.
creativeUses: Shocking scale: numbers that make the problem feel real and urgent
Progress/achievement: numbers that show how far we've come
Cost of inaction: what happens when you DON'T do the right thing
Comparative surprise: put two numbers next to each other that seem contradictory
components.when_to_use: For impactful statistics or numbers that deserve emphasis. Use 2-4 stats that tell a story together.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for non-numeric content. Don't use more than 4 stats. Don't use for numbers that aren't surprising or impactful.

variants.name: centered
variants.when_to_use: Default. Large centered numbers in a grid. Clean and impactful.
variants.when_not_to_use: Predictable if used every time.

variants.name: card-row
variants.when_to_use: Individual glass cards with progress bars underneath each stat. More visual weight and structure. Better when stats represent completion or progress.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for simple percentages where the bar adds no meaning.

required._items: array — 2-4 stats: [{ value: string, label: string, suffix?: string, prefix?: string }]

_items.label: Reduction in moving parts vs ICE

components.description: Emphasized text with a vertical accent bar and optional attribution. Draws attention to key messages or expert quotes.
components.learningMoment: A pullquote is a visual exclamation mark. It says: 'This is the one thing to remember from this section.' Used well, it becomes the line the learner quotes to their colleagues tomorrow.
creativeUses: The key takeaway that summarises everything above in one sentence
An expert voice that adds credibility and authority
A provocative reframing that shifts perspective
The 'so what?' — why this matters to the learner personally
components.when_to_use: For highlighting a critical insight, expert quote, or key message. Use sparingly — 1-2 per course maximum.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't overuse. Don't use for generic text that doesn't deserve emphasis.

variants.name: accent-bar
variants.when_to_use: Default. Vertical accent bar on the left with large italic quote text. Classic editorial look.
variants.when_not_to_use: Predictable if every pullquote uses this.

variants.name: centered
variants.when_to_use: Centered text with decorative quotation marks above and below. More dramatic, statement-like. Better for provocative or aspirational quotes.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for long quotes — centered text is harder to read at length.

variants.name: minimal
variants.when_to_use: Clean text with no decorative elements — just larger font size and extra whitespace. Sophisticated, understated. Best when the words speak for themselves.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can look like a regular text block if the font size difference isn't clear enough. Don't use if you need visual emphasis.

required.body: string — The quote or key message text (plain text, not HTML)

example.body: The shift to electric vehicles isn't just about changing fuel sources — it's about fundamentally rethinking how we approach vehicle safety and maintenance.

components.description: Highlighted vocabulary term with its definition. Styled with a distinctive background and icon to stand out from body text.
components.learningMoment: Key terms are anchors — they give learners precise language to describe what they've learned. When someone learns the RIGHT word for a concept, they can search for it, discuss it, and build on it.
creativeUses: Introduce jargon AFTER teaching the concept, not before — 'what you just learned is called...'
Group related terms to show how vocabulary connects
Use as a reference card near the end of a section for quick review
Pair with a flashcard component later for spaced retrieval
components.when_to_use: For introducing domain-specific terminology. Can group 2-5 terms together. Don't use for every new word — only for terms the learner needs to remember.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for common words. Don't use for full paragraphs of explanation (use text instead). Consider weaving simple definitions into prose, comparisons, or flashcards instead.

variants.name: list
variants.when_to_use: Default. Vertical list with term highlighted and definition below. Clean, scannable, works for 1-5 terms.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel long with 5+ terms.

variants.name: card-grid
variants.when_to_use: Terms displayed as cards in a responsive grid. More visual, each term gets equal weight. Better for 3-5 terms of similar importance.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for single terms. Don't use with very long definitions — cards need concise text.

required._items: array — 1-5 terms: [{ term: string, definition: string }]

_items.definition: An uncontrolled chain reaction in battery cells where rising temperature causes further heat generation, potentially leading to fire or explosion.
The current battery charge level expressed as a percentage of total capacity, similar to a fuel gauge.
A system that recovers kinetic energy during deceleration by using the electric motor as a generator to recharge the battery.

components.description: Checkable items with completion tracking and a progress indicator. Items can be ticked off by the learner.
components.learningMoment: Checklists transform knowledge into action. Each checkbox is a commitment: 'I understand this, and I'll do it.' The progress indicator creates momentum — learners want to reach 100%.
creativeUses: Pre-task checklist: everything to verify before starting a procedure
Self-assessment: 'Check each item you already do' to surface gaps
Action plan: concrete next steps the learner commits to
Readiness check: 'Can you tick all of these? If not, review the section above'
components.when_to_use: For action lists, pre-task checklists, safety procedures, or any content where learners should confirm understanding of each item.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for informational lists without actionable items (use text with <ul>). Don't use for sequential steps (use timeline).

variants.name: standard
variants.when_to_use: Default. Simple checkbox list with progress bar. Clean, functional, works everywhere.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel plain for high-stakes or premium courses.

variants.name: card-style
variants.when_to_use: Each item is a visual card with checkbox. More visual weight, premium feel. Better for action plans or commitment lists where each item deserves attention.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for long lists (8+) — too many cards.

variants.name: numbered
variants.when_to_use: Numbered badges instead of checkboxes. Items must be completed in order. Best for sequential procedures or priority-ranked lists.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for non-sequential items where order doesn't matter.

required._items: array — 3-10 items: [{ text: string, detail?: string }]

optional.instruction: string — Helper text (e.g. 'Check each item as you complete it')

example.instruction: Confirm each item before beginning work on an EV.

_items.text: Insulated gloves rated to 1000V are available and inspected
_items.detail: Check for tears, holes, or signs of damage before each use.

_items.text: Vehicle is powered off and key is removed
_items.detail: Verify 'Ready' light on dashboard is off.

_items.text: High-voltage service disconnect is confirmed open
Area is clear of water and conductive materials
Emergency response plan is reviewed and understood

components.description: Horizontal tabbed interface where each tab reveals different content. Only one tab visible at a time.
components.learningMoment: Tabs let learners choose their own path through parallel content. The act of switching tabs feels like exploration — 'What's behind this one?' Each tab is a different lens on the same topic.
creativeUses: Perspectives: same situation viewed by different roles or stakeholders
Categories: types, levels, or classifications presented side by side
Before/During/After: temporal views of a process or change
Beginner/Intermediate/Advanced: same topic at different depths
components.when_to_use: For 2-6 categories of content that are alternatives (not sequential). Users choose which to view.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for sequential content (use narrative). Don't use with more than 6 tabs (use accordion). Don't nest tabs inside tabs.

variants.name: horizontal
variants.when_to_use: Default. Centered pill buttons above content panel. Clean, familiar pattern.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel crowded with 5+ tabs or long tab labels.

variants.name: vertical
variants.when_to_use: Sidebar buttons on left, content panel on right. Better for longer tab labels, many tabs (4-6), or when tab names are descriptive phrases.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use with only 2 tabs — looks unbalanced.

required._items: array — 2-6 tabs: [{ title: string, body: string (HTML), _graphic?: { large: string, alt: string } }]

optional.displayTitle: string — Section heading above the tabs

_items.title: Battery Pack
_items.body: The battery pack is the most expensive component, typically comprising 30-40% of the vehicle's total cost. It consists of modules containing thousands of individual lithium-ion cells.

_items.title: Electric Motor
_items.body: Modern EVs use permanent magnet or induction motors that convert electrical energy to mechanical motion. They deliver instant torque from zero RPM.

_items.title: Power Electronics
_items.body: The inverter converts DC battery power to AC for the motor. The onboard charger handles AC-to-DC conversion during charging.

components.description: Flip-card interaction showing a term or question on the front and the answer/definition on the back. Click to flip with 3D rotation animation.
components.learningMoment: The moment before the flip is the learning. The learner tries to recall the answer, feels uncertainty, then flips to confirm or correct. That retrieval attempt strengthens memory more than re-reading ever could.
creativeUses: Vocabulary review after a section that introduced several terms
Scenario fronts: 'What should you do if...' with expert answer on back
Myth-busting: common belief on front, truth on back
Quick recall: test yourself before moving to the next section
components.when_to_use: For vocabulary review, Q&A pairs, or any content where learners should try to recall before seeing the answer. Place AFTER the teaching section.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for first-time content delivery. Don't use for complex multi-paragraph content.

variants.name: grid
variants.when_to_use: Default. Cards displayed in a responsive grid (2-3 columns). Best for 4-8 cards — creates a visual overview the learner can scan.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel crowded with very long front/back text.

variants.name: single-large
variants.when_to_use: One large card at a time with prev/next navigation. Better for longer content on each card or when you want focused attention on each flip.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use with 3 or fewer cards — not enough to justify navigation.

required._items: array — 3-8 cards: [{ front: string, back: string }]

optional.instruction: string — Helper text (e.g. 'Click each card to reveal the answer')

example.instruction: Click each card to test your knowledge.

_items.front: What voltage range do EV batteries typically operate at?
_items.back: 400-800V DC, with some performance vehicles exceeding 800V.
A system that uses the electric motor as a generator during deceleration to convert kinetic energy back into stored electrical energy.

_items.front: What PPE is required for EV high-voltage work?
_items.back: Class 0 insulated gloves (rated to 1000V), safety glasses, and insulated tools.

components.description: An image with numbered hotspot markers at specific positions. Clicking a marker reveals a tooltip with information about that point.
components.learningMoment: Labeled images connect abstract knowledge to physical reality. When a learner clicks a hotspot on a diagram and reads what's there, they're building a spatial mental model — understanding not just WHAT something is, but WHERE it is.
creativeUses: Anatomy of a system: components labeled on a diagram
Spot the issues: markers highlight problems in a scenario image
Interactive infographic: key data points anchored to visual positions
Before/after overlay: markers explain what changed
components.when_to_use: For diagrams, equipment photos, or illustrations where specific parts need identification.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use if the image is simple enough for a caption (use graphic).

variants.name: numbered-dots
variants.when_to_use: Default. Numbered circular markers on the image, clicking reveals a tooltip. Classic hotspot pattern.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel cluttered with 8+ markers.

variants.name: side-panel
variants.when_to_use: Image on left with a scrollable list panel on right. Clicking a list item highlights the corresponding area on the image. Better for detailed descriptions per marker.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for images where spatial position matters more than description detail.

required._markers: array — 3-8 hotspots: [{ x: number (0-100 %), y: number (0-100 %), label: string, body: string }]

optional.instruction: string — Helper text (e.g. 'Click each marker to learn more')

example.instruction: Click each numbered marker to explore the components.

_graphic.alt: Cutaway view of an EV battery pack showing internal components

_markers.label: Battery Cells
_markers.body: Individual lithium-ion cells arranged in series and parallel to achieve target voltage and capacity.

_markers.label: Cooling System
_markers.body: Liquid cooling channels that maintain optimal cell temperature between 20-40°C.

_markers.label: BMS Module
_markers.body: The Battery Management System that monitors and balances all cells.

_markers.label: HV Connector
_markers.body: High-voltage connector with safety interlock that disconnects during service.

components.description: Connected workflow nodes showing a process from start to end. Each node has a title and description, connected by directional arrows.
components.learningMoment: Process flows make decision logic visible. Unlike a timeline (which is linear), a process flow shows that choices lead to different outcomes — it teaches THINKING, not just doing.
creativeUses: Decision tree: 'If X, do Y; if Z, do W'
Troubleshooting flow: systematic diagnosis path
Escalation process: when to handle vs when to escalate
System architecture: how components interact and data flows
components.when_to_use: For workflows, decision trees, or any process where the flow and connections matter. Use 3-6 nodes.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for simple linear steps (use timeline).

variants.name: vertical
variants.when_to_use: Default. Nodes stacked vertically with downward arrows. Works for any number of nodes (3-6). Clear top-to-bottom flow.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel like a timeline if nodes don't have decision branching.

variants.name: horizontal
variants.when_to_use: Nodes arranged left-to-right with horizontal arrows. Better for 3-4 nodes on desktop. Creates a pipeline/workflow feel.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use with 5+ nodes — overflows on smaller screens. Falls back to vertical on mobile.

required._nodes: array — 3-6 process nodes: [{ title: string, body: string, type?: 'start' | 'process' | 'decision' | 'end' }]

_nodes.title: Check Battery Level
_nodes.body: Read the current State of Charge on the dashboard.

_nodes.title: Below 20%?
_nodes.body: If SoC is below 20%, proceed to charge immediately.

_nodes.title: Select Charger Type
_nodes.body: Choose between Level 2 (if time available) or DC Fast Charge (if urgent).

_nodes.title: Connect & Charge
_nodes.body: Plug in, authenticate, and monitor charging progress via the app.

_nodes.title: Charged to 80%+
_nodes.body: Disconnect once target SoC is reached. Avoid routinely charging to 100%.

components.description: Grid or masonry layout of multiple images with lightbox zoom on click. Captions shown on hover.
components.learningMoment: Galleries create a sense of abundance and variety. Multiple images together help learners see patterns, differences, and real-world diversity that a single image can't convey.
creativeUses: Examples gallery: 'Here's what this looks like in practice' across different contexts
Equipment reference: visual identification guide
Before/after pairs: multiple transformations shown together
Warning signs gallery: visual examples of things to watch for
components.when_to_use: For showcasing multiple related images. Use when content genuinely benefits from showing multiple visuals. 3-8 images.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't fabricate many images just to fill a gallery. Only use when the content genuinely benefits from multiple visuals.

required._items: array — 3-8 images: [{ _graphic: { large: string, alt: string }, caption?: string }]

_items.caption: Type 1 / J1772 — Standard in North America
CHAdeMO — Japanese standard DC fast charging

components.description: Edge-to-edge full-width image with optional text overlay. Creates visual impact and section breaks. Parallax scroll effect.
components.learningMoment: Full-bleeds are emotional punctuation. They say: 'Pause. Feel this. The next section is different.' The best full-bleeds create a sense of transition — closing one chapter and opening another.
creativeUses: Section transition with a provocative question or statement as overlay
Dramatic reveal after a dense teaching section — let the learner breathe
Scale/impact image that makes an abstract concept feel real
Aspirational image: 'This is what success looks like'
components.when_to_use: For dramatic visual section dividers between major topics. 1-3 per course maximum.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't overuse. Don't use for images that need captions or detailed study.

variants.name: center
variants.when_to_use: Default. Text overlay centered on the image. Balanced, works for any message.
variants.when_not_to_use: If the image has a strong focal point on one side, the centered text may compete with it.

variants.name: left
variants.when_to_use: Text positioned on the left third with gradient from left. Better when the image's focal point is on the right side.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use if the left side of the image contains the important visual content.

variants.name: right
variants.when_to_use: Text positioned on the right third with gradient from right. Mirror of left variant.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use if the right side of the image contains the important visual content.

example.body: As EV adoption accelerates, your expertise in safety and maintenance becomes increasingly vital.

_graphic.alt: Fleet of electric vehicles on a modern highway at sunset

components.description: Video player with an expandable transcript panel below. Combines media playback with accessible text content.
components.learningMoment: Combining video with transcript respects different learning preferences — some people watch, some read, some do both. The transcript also makes content searchable and accessible.
creativeUses: Expert interview with full text for reference
Demonstration video with step-by-step written procedure alongside
Real-world footage with analysis commentary in transcript
components.when_to_use: Only when both a video file AND its transcript text are available.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use if there's no video (use text). Don't use if there's no transcript (use media).

example.transcript:
Interviewer: Can you explain the safety systems built into modern EV batteries?

Dr. Chen: Absolutely. Modern battery packs have multiple layers of protection...

components.description: Persistent course path selector. Learners choose their role/difficulty/audience, and subsequent sections show/hide based on the selection.
components.learningMoment: Path selection gives learners ownership of their experience. Choosing a path is an act of self-identification — 'I am this kind of learner' — which increases engagement because the content now feels personally relevant.
creativeUses: Role-based: 'I'm a manager' vs 'I'm a developer' vs 'I'm in compliance'
Experience-based: 'I'm new to this' vs 'I have some experience' vs 'I'm refreshing'
Context-based: 'I work in healthcare' vs 'I work in finance' vs 'I work in tech'
components.when_to_use: When content-bucket.json contains pathGroups (SCORM import path only). Place immediately after the hero.
components.when_not_to_use: Never use for educational branching scenarios (use branching component). Only use when SCORM extraction detected a path-selection pattern.

required._items: array — One item per path option: { title: string, body: string, variable: string }

optional.body: string (HTML) — Intro text above the selector cards
optional.pathGroup: string — Name of the path group from content-bucket.json

example.body: Select your role to see content tailored to your responsibilities.

components.description: Visual break between sections or content groups. Can be a horizontal line, whitespace, or a centered icon.
components.learningMoment: Dividers are invisible architecture. They tell the learner's brain: 'That topic is done. Something new is coming.' Without them, dense courses blur together.
creativeUses: Use a spacing divider between two dense sections for cognitive breathing room
Use an icon divider to signal a topic shift (lightbulb for insight, warning for caution)
Use a line divider inside a section to separate sub-topics without starting a new section
components.when_to_use: Between sections or content groups that need visual separation. Use sparingly — 2-4 per course maximum. The AI generation engine should place dividers at natural topic transitions.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use between every section — sections already have spacing. Don't use inside interactive components. Don't use as decoration.

variants.name: line
variants.when_to_use: Default. Subtle horizontal rule with opacity. Clean, minimal separation.
variants.when_not_to_use: Can feel clinical if overused.

variants.name: spacing
variants.when_to_use: Extra whitespace only — no visible element. For cognitive breathing room between heavy sections.
variants.when_not_to_use: Invisible, so don't use where the learner needs a clear signal of topic change.

variants.name: icon
variants.when_to_use: Centered icon (from Material Symbols) with thin lines on either side. Signals a topic shift with personality.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use if there's no natural icon for the transition. Don't use more than once per course.

optional.style: string — 'line' (default), 'spacing', or 'icon'. Must match the variant name.
optional.icon: string — Material Symbols icon name (only for icon style, e.g. 'lightbulb', 'warning', 'rocket_launch')

components.description: Styled notification box with icon, title, and body. Used for tips, warnings, important notes, or success messages.
components.learningMoment: Callouts are attention anchors — they break the reading flow intentionally to say 'This is important enough to highlight.' The icon and colour create an instant emotional signal before the text is even read.
creativeUses: Tip callout after a complex section: 'Pro tip: the shortcut most people miss is...'
Warning callout before a common mistake: 'Watch out: this is where 80% of errors happen'
Success callout to celebrate progress: 'You've just learned the hardest concept in this course'
Info callout for supplementary context that enriches but isn't essential
components.when_to_use: For content that deserves visual emphasis — tips, warnings, important notes, or success celebrations. Use 2-5 per course.
components.when_not_to_use: Don't use for regular content that doesn't need highlighting. Don't cluster multiple callouts together. Don't use as a substitute for good heading hierarchy.

variants.name: info
variants.when_to_use: Default. Blue/neutral accent. For supplementary context, additional detail, or 'good to know' notes.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for warnings or critical information.

variants.name: warning
variants.when_to_use: Amber/orange accent with warning icon. For common mistakes, pitfalls, or 'watch out' moments.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't overuse — if everything is a warning, nothing is.

variants.name: tip
variants.when_to_use: Green accent with lightbulb icon. For pro tips, shortcuts, best practices, or insider knowledge.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use for basic information that isn't actually a tip.

variants.name: success
variants.when_to_use: Green/positive accent with check icon. For celebrating milestones, confirming understanding, or positive reinforcement.
variants.when_not_to_use: Don't use at the start of a course — save for after the learner has achieved something.

optional.calloutType: string — 'info' (default), 'warning', 'tip', 'success' — determines icon and colour accent

example.body: Never attempt to disconnect the high-voltage battery without first verifying the vehicle is fully powered down. The 'Ready' indicator light on the dashboard must be off.

design_rules.visual_variety: Never place two consecutive components of the same type. Alternate between content-heavy and visual components.
design_rules.section_rhythm: Vary section density intentionally. Breather sections (1-2 components) give the learner space. Deep dive sections (5-7 components) go thorough. Standard sections (3-4 components) are the workhorse.
design_rules.image_alternation: When using multiple graphic-text components, alternate _imageAlign between 'left' and 'right'.
design_rules.assessment_spacing: Don't scatter MCQs after every section. Cluster assessments into 2-3 checkpoint moments.
design_rules.hero_rule: Every course starts with exactly one hero component.
design_rules.visual_breaks: Use full-bleed, stat-callout, or pullquote between dense content sections to create breathing room.
design_rules.scroll_test: For every section, ask: 'Would a learner on their phone scroll past this at 11pm?' If yes, redesign it.

Text.label: Text
Text.description: Text blocks, quotes, callouts, and glossary terms
Text.question: I want to present text or key messages

Image.label: Image
Image.question: I want to show images or pair images with text

Explore.label: Explore
Explore.description: Content the learner reveals by clicking, tapping, or navigating
Explore.question: I want the learner to discover content at their own pace

Quiz.label: Quiz
Quiz.description: Test understanding, prompt reflection, or track progress
Quiz.question: I want to check understanding or prompt action

Layout.label: Layout
Layout.description: Arrange multiple items in grids, sequences, tables, or flows
Layout.question: I have several related items to display together

Media.label: Media
Media.question: I have video or audio content

Structure.label: Structure
Structure.description: Course framing, navigation, and visual breaks
Structure.question: I need a course opener or visual break
